refined_fr.refined_fr: Capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model.
refined_fr.rationale: The candidate FR bundles three distinct obligations: (1) terrain analysis, (2) obstacle identification, and (3) work-zone representation. Each is a separate functional concern with different data sources, processing logic, and stakeholder consumption patterns. The refined FR is solution-neutral (avoids 'mapping/modeling' terminology which implies implementation choice) and focuses on the core problem: making worksite geometry and features available in a structured, queryable form. Splitting into atomic sub-FRs allows independent DP selection and IM packaging.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Confirm that a worksite model exists in a defined schema (e.g., point cloud, mesh, or feature database) and can be queried by downstream planning and execution functions to retrieve spatial attributes (position, geometry, classification) for an agreed set of site features.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-006.1: Acquire terrain elevation and surface geometry data from the worksite.
FR-NGHE-006.2: Identify and classify static obstacles (structures, vegetation, utilities) within the worksite boundary.
FR-NGHE-006.3: Define and represent work-zone boundaries and restricted areas in the spatial model.
FR-NGHE-006.4: Persist and provide access to the worksite spatial model for planning and execution functions.

candidate_dps: DP1: Acquire spatial feature data via multi-modal sensing (vision, range, inertial) and fuse into a unified coordinate frame; store as a structured spatial graph with feature attributes (geometry, classification, safety zone) queryable by planning/execution functions.

DP2: Decompose worksite into discrete zones; assign feature capture responsibility to zone-specific acquisition modules; merge zone models via spatial registration and conflict resolution; expose unified model through a feature query interface.

DP3: Implement a real-time streaming pipeline that ingests raw sensor observations, applies incremental feature extraction and classification, and maintains a versioned spatial database; provide read-only snapshots and change notifications to downstream consumers.

DP4: Establish a declarative feature schema (geometry primitives, safety attributes, operational constraints); acquire raw spatial data; apply schema-driven extraction and validation; persist as a normalized model with audit trail; serve via standardized query API.

DP5: Delegate feature capture to autonomous agents (mobile platforms, drones) that operate under safety policies; aggregate observations into a consensus spatial model; resolve conflicts via temporal voting or confidence weighting; expose model with uncertainty quantification.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP4: Establish a declarative feature schema (geometry primitives, safety attributes, operational constraints); acquire raw spatial data; apply schema-driven extraction and validation; persist as a normalized model with audit trail; serve via standardized query API.

potential_sources: Schema evolution: changes to feature definitions may require re-processing historical data; mitigate via versioning and backward-compatible schema extensions.

Acquisition method coupling: DP4 remains agnostic to sensing modality (vision, LiDAR, manual annotation) but assumes data arrives in a normalized form; risk if acquisition pipeline cannot reliably produce schema-conformant observations.

Planning/execution coupling: downstream functions depend on query API contract; changes to model structure or query semantics could break consumers; mitigate via API versioning and deprecation policy.

Safety policy coupling: feature schema must encode safety-relevant attributes (exclusion zones, hazard classifications); changes to safety policies may require schema updates; mitigate via extensible attribute framework.
coupling_check.notes: DP4 achieves good separation of concerns: schema definition (logical), data acquisition (sensing/input), model persistence (storage), and consumption (query interface) are distinct concerns. Coupling is primarily at schema boundaries and API contracts, which are manageable via versioning and governance. DP4 is superior to DP1 (which conflates fusion logic with storage) and DP3 (which couples real-time streaming to model structure). DP2 and DP5 introduce spatial decomposition and agent coordination complexity that may be premature without clear scalability drivers.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Raw spatial data (from sensors, manual input, or third-party sources) can be reliably transformed into schema-conformant observations with acceptable latency and accuracy.
RISK: If acquisition sources are heterogeneous or unreliable, validation and conflict resolution logic may become complex; recommend establishing data quality SLAs and fallback mechanisms.
ASSUMPTION: Feature schema is stable enough to support planning/execution functions without frequent breaking changes.

RISK: If worksite conditions or safety policies evolve rapidly, schema versioning and migration overhead may become significant; recommend periodic schema review cycles aligned with operational planning.
ASSUMPTION: Query API is sufficient to support all anticipated planning and execution use cases (e.g., spatial queries, feature filtering, uncertainty retrieval).
RISK: If downstream functions require complex analytical queries or real-time streaming updates, a read-only snapshot model may be insufficient; recommend prototyping query patterns early.
ASSUMPTION: Audit trail and model versioning are required for safety compliance and operational transparency.
RISK: Storage and query performance may degrade with large historical datasets; recommend archival strategy and performance testing.
ASSUMPTION: Safety policies and operational constraints can be encoded as feature attributes and validated at model persistence time.
RISK: If policies are context-dependent or dynamic, static schema encoding may be insufficient; recommend policy engine integration or dynamic constraint evaluation.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP4 to explicitly separate concerns: (1) Schema Definition: establish a versioned, extensible feature schema that encodes geometry, classification, safety attributes, and operational constraints; (2) Acquisition & Validation: define data quality requirements and validation rules for incoming observations; establish fallback/conflict resolution logic; (3) Persistence: implement a normalized spatial database with audit trail and versioning; (4) Query Interface: expose a read-only API with support for spatial queries, feature filtering, and uncertainty retrieval; include API versioning and deprecation policy. Recommend prototyping query patterns with planning/execution functions early to validate schema sufficiency. Consider introducing a policy engine or constraint evaluation layer if safety policies are dynamic or context-dependent. Establish data quality SLAs and monitoring to detect acquisition failures early.

im_boundary.name: Safety Services
im_boundary.scope: Centralized module responsible for declarative safety schema definition, spatial data acquisition and validation, normalized model persistence with audit trail, and standardized query API for safety-critical feature extraction and constraint enforcement.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Inbound raw spatial data (geometry primitives, sensor readings, operational state) from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems for schema-driven extraction and validation.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Outbound safety constraint violations, audit trail events, and validated feature state queries to dependent consumers (Vehicle, Teleoperation, NGHE Solution).

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional interface for operational constraint queries (inbound) and constraint-aware task feasibility responses (outbound).

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: schema definition, validation logic, audit trail, and query API are all safety-domain concerns. Raw data acquisition via Telemetry interface is well-scoped. Minor diffusion: normalized model persistence may require coordination with external data layer, but core logic remains contained.

Poor fit: NGHE Solution is a top-level orchestrator/coordinator. Embedding schema, validation, and persistence here violates separation of concerns. Safety logic would diffuse across multiple subsystems and become entangled with mission planning. High coupling risk.

Poor fit: Vehicle is a physical/hardware abstraction layer. Safety schema, validation, and audit trail are cross-cutting concerns that should not be vehicle-specific. Persistence and query API would be misplaced in a hardware module. High diffusion to Teleoperation and Safety Services.

Poor fit: Teleoperation is an operator interface and control pathway. While it consumes safety constraints, it should not own schema definition, validation, or audit trail logic. Safety logic would diffuse to Vehicle and Safety Services. Violates single responsibility.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Safety Services is the natural owner of DP4 because: (1) declarative feature schema and safety attributes are inherently safety-domain concerns; (2) validation and constraint enforcement are core safety responsibilities; (3) audit trail is a safety/compliance requirement; (4) standardized query API serves as the safety contract for all consumers. The module boundary cleanly separates safety logic from vehicle control (Vehicle), operator interaction (Teleoperation), and mission orchestration (NGHE Solution).
containment_risks: Risk: Raw spatial data acquisition may require real-time streaming from multiple sensor sources (Vehicle, Teleoperation). If Telemetry interface does not provide sufficient bandwidth or latency guarantees, validation latency could impact safety-critical decisions.
Risk: Normalized model persistence (audit trail) may require external database or storage service. If not properly abstracted, coupling to a specific persistence technology could reduce modularity.
Risk: Query API may become a bottleneck if many consumers (Vehicle, Teleoperation, NGHE Solution) poll for constraint validation synchronously. Async SafetyAlert notifications should be preferred.
Risk: Schema evolution (adding new geometry primitives or safety attributes) requires coordinated updates across all consumers. Versioning strategy must be explicit.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: DP4 is well-formulated and maps cleanly to Safety Services. No revision needed. However, clarify that: (1) raw spatial data acquisition is a pull operation via Telemetry interface (not a push responsibility of Safety Services); (2) persistence is abstracted behind a repository pattern to decouple from specific storage technology; (3) query API should support both synchronous (for validation checks) and asynchronous (SafetyAlert) modes to avoid blocking.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. Acceptance criteria require that a worksite model is generated and available to planning/execution functions. Safety Services module provides this via standardized query API (synchronous) and SafetyAlert notifications (asynchronous). Traceability is clear: FR (capture and represent worksite spatial features) -> DP4 (declarative schema, acquisition, validation, persistence, query API) -> Safety Services (IM). Audit trail enables verification of feature extraction and constraint enforcement compliance with worksite safety plan and operational policies.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Excellent. Safety Services ownership is unambiguous: schema definition, validation logic, audit trail, and query API are all safety-domain concerns with no competing ownership claims. External interfaces (Telemetry inbound, SafetyAlert outbound, Tasking bidirectional) clearly delineate responsibilities. Raw data acquisition is a pull operation via Telemetry, not a Safety Services responsibility. Persistence is abstracted, reducing coupling to specific storage technology.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP4 comprehensively addresses FR: (1) declarative feature schema captures geometry primitives and safety attributes required to represent worksite spatial features; (2) schema-driven extraction and validation ensure machine-readable model correctness; (3) normalized model with audit trail provides traceability for safety plan compliance; (4) standardized query API makes model available to planning/execution functions. DP is solution-neutral and does not over-specify implementation details (e.g., specific database technology, sensor protocols). Logical coherence is strong.

diffusion_risks: Risk: Real-time streaming latency. Telemetry interface must guarantee sufficient bandwidth and latency bounds for safety-critical validation. If raw spatial data acquisition blocks validation, constraint enforcement delays could violate safety-critical decision timelines. Mitigation: Define SLA for Telemetry interface (e.g., max 100ms end-to-end latency for geometry updates).

Risk: Persistence abstraction leakage. If normalized model persistence is tightly coupled to a specific database technology (e.g., PostgreSQL, MongoDB), schema evolution and failover become brittle. Mitigation: Enforce repository pattern; persistence implementation must be swappable without changing Safety Services public interfaces.

Risk: Query API bottleneck. Synchronous polling by multiple consumers (Vehicle, Teleoperation, NGHE Solution) could create contention and latency spikes. Mitigation: Prefer asynchronous SafetyAlert notifications for constraint violations; reserve synchronous query API for validation checks only, with explicit timeout handling.

Risk: Schema versioning and evolution. Adding new geometry primitives or safety attributes requires coordinated updates across all consumers. Without explicit versioning strategy, breaking changes could cascade. Mitigation: Implement schema versioning (e.g., semantic versioning); SafetyAlert and query API responses must include schema version; consumers must declare supported versions.

Risk: Minor diffusion in normalized model persistence. Coordination with external data layer (if present) could introduce coupling. However, containment score of 0.92 indicates this is well-managed via abstraction.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment. Safety Services is the natural owner of DP4. All core responsibilities (schema definition, validation, audit trail, query API) are safety-domain concerns and remain within module boundary. Raw data acquisition is properly scoped as an inbound interface (Telemetry), not an internal responsibility. Separation of concerns is clean: Vehicle handles hardware abstraction, Teleoperation handles operator interaction, NGHE Solution handles mission orchestration, and Safety Services owns safety logic. No competing ownership claims. Diffusion score of 0.08 is acceptable; minor coordination with persistence layer is expected and manageable via abstraction.

recommended_revisions: Clarify Telemetry interface SLA: Define maximum latency and minimum throughput for raw spatial data streaming to ensure validation does not block safety-critical decisions.

Enforce repository pattern for persistence: Decouple Safety Services from specific database technology. Persistence implementation must be swappable (e.g., via dependency injection or adapter pattern).

Implement dual-mode query API: Support both synchronous (for validation checks with explicit timeout) and asynchronous (SafetyAlert notifications for constraint violations). Document when each mode should be used.

Define schema versioning strategy: Implement semantic versioning for declarative feature schema. SafetyAlert and query API responses must include schema version. Consumers must declare supported versions to enable graceful degradation during schema evolution.

Add explicit audit trail requirements: Specify what events must be logged (e.g., schema updates, validation failures, constraint violations, query access), retention period, and compliance obligations (e.g., GDPR, safety regulations).

Document failure modes: Define behavior when Telemetry interface is unavailable, persistence fails, or query API times out. Ensure safety-critical constraints are enforced even under degraded conditions.

decision.justification: Gate results confirm FR4 is well-formed (FR-DP-IM alignment is sound with high verification feasibility and excellent allocation clarity). However, recommended revisions expose critical design details that must be decomposed into explicit sub-FRs to ensure safety-critical requirements are properly traced, allocated, and verified. Specifically: (1) Telemetry interface SLA and latency bounds are safety-critical and require dedicated FR-DP-IM treatment; (2) persistence abstraction and repository pattern are architectural concerns that need explicit functional decomposition; (3) dual-mode query API (synchronous + asynchronous) and schema versioning are complex enough to warrant sub-level analysis; (4) audit trail and failure mode handling are safety-compliance obligations that must be traceable to design decisions. High safety criticality tailoring factor reinforces need for explicit decomposition rather than deferring to implementation. Proceeding to next level will ensure all recommended revisions are captured as formal requirements with clear ownership and verification criteria.
next_level_sub_frs: FR4.1: Ensure real-time spatial data acquisition from Telemetry interface meets safety-critical latency and throughput bounds without blocking constraint validation.
FR4.2: Provide persistence abstraction that decouples Safety Services schema and validation logic from specific database technology, enabling swappable implementations.
FR4.3: Support dual-mode query API (synchronous validation checks with timeout handling; asynchronous SafetyAlert notifications for constraint violations) with clear usage semantics.
FR4.4: Implement declarative feature schema versioning strategy that enables graceful evolution and consumer compatibility negotiation without breaking changes.
FR4.5: Maintain comprehensive audit trail of schema updates, validation failures, constraint violations, and query access with compliance-mandated retention and traceability.
FR4.6: Enforce safety-critical constraint validation and alert generation even under degraded conditions (Telemetry unavailable, persistence failure, query API timeout).

revise.why: DP4 is logically sound but insufficiently detailed to guide safe implementation. Recommended revisions must be elevated to explicit DP statements at next level: (1) DP4.1 must specify Telemetry SLA (e.g., max 100ms latency, min throughput), buffering strategy, and backpressure handling; (2) DP4.2 must define repository pattern and persistence abstraction boundaries; (3) DP4.3 must specify dual-mode API contract (synchronous timeout semantics, asynchronous notification guarantees); (4) DP4.4 must define semantic versioning scheme and schema evolution rules; (5) DP4.5 must specify audit event taxonomy, retention policy, and compliance mappings; (6) DP4.6 must define failure mode handling and safety-critical fallback logic. These DPs will be derived during next-level decomposition and will inform implementation module design.

assumptions: Telemetry interface is external to Safety Services and provides raw spatial data via streaming API; Safety Services does not own raw data acquisition.
Persistence layer is abstracted and can be swapped (e.g., relational DB, document store, time-series DB) without changing Safety Services public interfaces.
Query API consumers (Vehicle, Teleoperation, NGHE Solution) can tolerate asynchronous SafetyAlert notifications for constraint violations; synchronous queries are reserved for validation checks only.
Schema versioning is feasible and necessary to support graceful evolution; consumers can declare supported schema versions.
Audit trail is a compliance obligation (safety regulations, operational traceability) and must be enforced at Safety Services level, not deferred to persistence layer.
Failure modes (Telemetry unavailable, persistence failure, query timeout) are credible and must be explicitly handled to maintain safety-critical constraint enforcement.
open_risks: Telemetry interface SLA not yet defined: If latency bounds are not contractually enforced, validation could block safety-critical decisions. Mitigation: Derive FR4.1 and DP4.1 with explicit SLA and backpressure handling.

Persistence abstraction leakage: If repository pattern is not enforced during implementation, Safety Services could become tightly coupled to specific database technology. Mitigation: Derive FR4.2 and DP4.2 with explicit abstraction boundaries and dependency injection requirements.
Query API contention: Synchronous polling by multiple consumers could create latency spikes. Mitigation: Derive FR4.3 and DP4.3 with dual-mode semantics and explicit timeout handling.

Schema versioning complexity: Without explicit versioning strategy, breaking changes could cascade to consumers. Mitigation: Derive FR4.4 and DP4.4 with semantic versioning scheme and compatibility negotiation rules.

Audit trail compliance gaps: If audit trail requirements are not explicitly specified, compliance obligations (GDPR, safety regulations) could be missed. Mitigation: Derive FR4.5 and DP4.5 with explicit event taxonomy and retention policy.

Failure mode handling undefined: If Safety Services does not explicitly handle degraded conditions, safety-critical constraints could be violated. Mitigation: Derive FR4.6 and DP4.6 with explicit failure mode handling and fallback logic.
evidence_needed: Telemetry interface specification: Define SLA (latency, throughput), data format, streaming protocol, backpressure semantics.
Persistence abstraction design: Define repository pattern, persistence interface contract, swappability requirements, technology-agnostic schema representation.
Query API specification: Define synchronous API contract (timeout semantics, error handling), asynchronous notification contract (SafetyAlert format, delivery guarantees), usage guidelines.
Schema versioning strategy: Define semantic versioning scheme, schema evolution rules, version negotiation protocol, backward compatibility policy.

Audit trail specification: Define event taxonomy (schema updates, validation failures, constraint violations, query access), retention period, compliance mappings (GDPR, safety regulations), access control.
Failure mode analysis: Define credible failure scenarios (Telemetry unavailable, persistence failure, query timeout), safety-critical fallback logic, graceful degradation strategy.

Integration test plan: Verify that sub-FRs (FR4.1-FR4.6) are satisfied by corresponding DPs and IMs; verify that recommended revisions are addressed; verify that safety-critical constraints are enforced under nominal and degraded conditions.
